Once dry, store your jumpers folded, never hung, to avoid shoulder marks and stretching. Fold them upright in a drawer so you can see them and keep their shape.

sections.heading: Dealing with pilling
sections.body: Pilling — those little fibre balls — is normal on wool, especially at friction points. It doesn't mean poor quality. A fabric shaver or special comb removes them in minutes and makes the jumper look new again. Do it regularly rather than waiting. Well-maintained and depilled, a jumper keeps its good looks for a very long time.

steps: Wash wool cold, on a wool cycle or by hand
Don't wring — press to remove water
Dry flat on a towel, never hung
Store jumpers folded, not on a hanger
Remove pilling with a fabric shaver

faq.q: My jumper shrank — can it be saved?
faq.a: Sometimes. Soak it in lukewarm water with a little conditioner, then gently stretch it flat. No guarantee, but worth trying — prevention is better.

faq.q: Should you wash wool often?
faq.a: No — wool naturally resists odours. Airing a jumper is usually enough; an occasional gentle wash is plenty.

title: Dressing to present a project or give a talk
intro: On the day you present to an audience, your outfit supports your credibility. The right choice also puts you more at ease.

sections.heading: Your outfit reinforces your message
sections.body: When you're presenting a project, a talk, or a dissertation, the audience looks at you as much as it listens — especially in the first few minutes. A polished outfit sends a signal of seriousness and preparation, which lends credibility to what you're saying before you even speak. You don't need to overdo it: a neat outfit, one notch above your daily norm, is enough to show you're taking the exercise seriously.

sections.heading: Comfort to stay focused
sections.body: Presenting in public already demands a lot of concentration — your outfit shouldn't add to it. Avoid anything that constricts, pinches, or that you've never worn before. You might get warm under pressure: prefer a breathable fabric and a colour that doesn't show sweat. An outfit that makes you feel good frees your mind for the content.

sections.heading: Simplicity and readability
sections.body: In front of an audience, a readable outfit that doesn't distract is better. Avoid very busy patterns, accessories that clash or jingle, and loud colours. A simple base with optionally one personal colour touch is enough. The goal is for the audience to remember your message and your confidence, not a wardrobe detail. Clean simplicity is your ally.

steps: Aim for one notch above your daily norm
Choose comfort to stay focused
Prefer breathable, sweat-resistant fabrics
Avoid busy patterns and noisy accessories
Prepare and test your outfit before the day

faq.q: Do you have to wear formal clothes to give a talk?
faq.a: Not always. Adapt to the context — school, university, company. A neat, polished outfit, even casual, is usually enough.

faq.q: Does your outfit affect how the audience perceives you?
faq.a: Yes, especially in the first few minutes. A polished outfit lends credibility and reinforces your confidence in front of the audience.

title: Choosing a jacket that goes with everything
intro: A good jacket structures any outfit. It's one of the most cost-effective pieces in a wardrobe.

sections.heading: The structuring role of a jacket
sections.body: A jacket is the third piece that transforms an outfit: thrown over a simple top and bottoms, it instantly gives intention and edge. It's what makes the difference between an outfit that feels unfinished and one that's considered. Having a versatile jacket means being able to elevate any combination in one move — from everyday to occasion.

sections.heading: The most versatile models
sections.body: Several jacket types are highly versatile. An unstructured blazer works from casual to smart. A denim jacket is an easy classic, perfect in transitional seasons. A leather jacket adds character. An overshirt is the most relaxed option. Choose based on your style, but prioritise a neutral colour — navy, black, beige, grey — that goes with the majority of your wardrobe.

sections.heading: Fit above all
sections.body: A jacket is chosen first by its shoulders: the shoulder seam should fall exactly at the edge of your shoulder — that's the hardest area to alter. Sleeves end at the wrist. The jacket closes without pulling and gives you room to move your arms. A well-cut jacket, even a cheap one, has more style than an expensive jacket that doesn't fit. Get it altered if needed.

steps: Choose a model suited to your style
Prioritise a neutral, versatile colour
Check the shoulder line first
Check sleeve length and ease of movement
Get it altered if the fit is almost perfect

faq.q: Can one jacket be enough?
faq.a: Yes to start. A neutral blazer or denim jacket already covers a lot of situations. You add more based on your needs.

faq.q: Which jacket should you buy first?
faq.a: An unstructured blazer or a denim jacket in a neutral colour. Both move easily from casual to smart.

title: Dressing well whatever your body type
intro: No body type is an obstacle to style. Every silhouette can be highlighted and celebrated.

sections.heading: No body is a problem
sections.body: The fashion industry has long suggested you need a specific body type to dress well. That's false. Every body type, every size, can wear stunning outfits. Style has no ideal weight or size. The goal is never to hide or transform your body — it's to find clothes in which you feel good and yourself.

sections.heading: Prioritise comfort and fit
sections.body: Whatever your silhouette, the principle is the same: a garment in your actual size — not too tight, not too loose — in a pleasant fabric. A well-cut piece always looks good on you. Ignore the displayed size and trust what you feel and see in the mirror. A piece you love that makes you feel at ease is made for you — full stop.

sections.heading: Wearing what you love
sections.body: There are lots of tips about what supposedly does or doesn't work for certain body types. Take them as suggestions, never as prohibitions. If a colour, cut, or piece appeals to you and makes you happy, you have every right to wear it — no conditions. The confidence and joy you feel in an outfit shows more than any rule. Your body has nothing to prove.

steps: Remember that no body type is a flaw
Choose clothes in your actual size
Trust comfort and the mirror, not the label
Take body type rules as suggestions
Wear what you love and what makes you happy

faq.q: Are some clothes forbidden based on body type?
faq.a: No. No piece is off-limits. Body type advice is optional, not rules. If it appeals to you and feels right in your head, go for it.

faq.q: Does style depend on body size?
faq.a: No. Every body type can be beautifully dressed. What counts is the right fit, comfort, and the joy of wearing it.

title: Creating a style mood board
intro: A mood board gathers what visually attracts you and gradually reveals your style. It's a simple and powerful tool.

sections.heading: What a mood board is for
sections.body: An inspiration board is a collection of images you love: outfits, vibes, colours, attitudes. Its purpose isn't copying — it's surfacing patterns. When you gather enough images, themes emerge — colours, cuts, a vibe — that map your actual taste. It's a mirror of your preferences that's often more honest than what you think you like.

sections.heading: Building it
sections.body: Choose a format: a pinning app, a photo folder, or an actual physical board with printed images. Over a few weeks, save everything that draws you in — no censoring, no overthinking. Aim for around fifty images. Don't limit yourself to outfits: a mood, a place, a colour can all inspire. The spontaneity of collecting is what makes it revealing.

sections.heading: Analysing and using it
sections.body: Once you have enough images, look at them together. What colours keep coming up? What cuts? What overall attitude? These patterns are the pillars of your style. Use them concretely: when you're shopping or sorting your wardrobe, keep your mood board in mind. It becomes a compass that guides your choices and saves you from off-brand purchases.

steps: Choose a format: app, folder, or physical board
Collect 50 images that draw you in, without censoring
Include moods and colours, not just outfits
Spot the patterns: colours, cuts, vibe
Keep this mood board in mind when shopping

faq.q: How long until a style pattern appears?
faq.a: A few weeks of collecting is enough. By 40-50 images, patterns become visible. The more spontaneously you collect, the more revealing it is.

faq.q: Should you copy the images in your mood board?
faq.a: No. The mood board is for spotting what you like, not reproducing an outfit. You draw out patterns to build your own style.

title: Wearing a suit or tailored set properly
intro: A well-worn suit commands respect; a poorly fitted one undermines you. A few simple rules make all the difference.

sections.heading: Fit: the decisive factor
sections.body: A suit lives or dies by its fit. The jacket's shoulder seam must stop exactly at the edge of your shoulder. When buttoned, the jacket shouldn't pull — and a little shirt cuff should show at the sleeve. The trousers fall just onto the shoe without excess fabric. A cheap suit that fits well beats an expensive suit that's badly cut. Alterations are your best friend here.

sections.heading: The rules of wearing it
sections.body: A few classic codes make a suit look sharp. On a two-button jacket, button the top button when standing and unbutton everything when sitting. A tie, if you wear one, ends at the middle of your belt buckle. Belt and shoes match in colour. These details are noticed and signal that you know what you're doing.

sections.heading: Adapting and personalising
sections.body: A suit isn't just a rigid uniform. You can soften it: worn without a tie, collar open, or the jacket alone with different trousers. A sober colour — navy or grey — is more versatile than black, which is often reserved for formal occasions. A discreet pocket square or colourful socks add a personal touch. A well-chosen suit can read as both dressed-up or casual depending on how you wear it.

steps: Get the suit altered, especially the shoulders
Button the right button, unbutton when sitting
Match belt and shoes in colour
Go for navy or grey over black
Personalise with a subtle touch

faq.q: Which suit should you buy first?
faq.a: A plain navy or grey suit, well-fitted. More versatile than black, it works for interviews, weddings, work, and can be separated.

faq.q: Do you always need a tie with a suit?
faq.a: No. A suit can be worn open-collar, without a tie, for a more modern and relaxed effect depending on the context.

title: Dressing for a festival or concert
intro: A festival combines style, comfort, and endurance. The right outfit lets you enjoy it without suffering.

sections.heading: Comfort and endurance first
sections.body: A festival means being on your feet, walking and dancing for hours, often in heat or dust. Go for breathable clothes and closed, comfortable shoes that aren't new — your feet will thank you. Avoid anything that constricts or that you'd hate to damage. An outfit you can move freely in keeps your focus on the music and the atmosphere.

sections.heading: Planning for the weather
sections.body: Outdoors, conditions shift: hot in the afternoon, cool in the evening, sometimes rain. Layer up: a light base and a piece to add when the temperature drops. A lightweight waterproof or compact wind jacket in your bag saves you from a shower. A cap or hat protects you from the sun. Thinking about the whole day stops you getting caught off guard.

sections.heading: Style that's bold and practical
sections.body: A festival is the chance to go for it — colours, accessories, fun pieces you wouldn't dare wear elsewhere. Enjoy it, it's the right setting. But stay practical: a small bag that closes well to keep your phone and keys safe, useful pockets. Avoid valuable jewellery in the crowd. The ideal is an outfit that's true to you, fun, and holds up all day.

steps: Choose closed shoes that are already broken in
Layer up for changing weather
Pack a light waterproof and sun protection
Go bold with colours and fun accessories
Bring a small bag that closes securely

faq.q: Can you be stylish and comfortable at a festival?
faq.a: Absolutely. A festival is actually the perfect setting for a fun, colourful style — as long as you prioritise pieces you can move in.

faq.q: What shoes for a festival?
faq.a: Closed, comfortable shoes that are already broken in. You'll be walking and dancing for hours, sometimes in dust or mud.

title: Removing stains effectively
intro: A stain isn't the end. Acting fast with the right method saves most pieces.

sections.heading: Act fast — the key reflex
sections.body: A fresh stain almost always comes out; a dried, set-in one resists. As soon as a stain appears, act: blot, don't rub. Blot from the edges toward the centre with a clean cloth to absorb without spreading. Avoid hot water on unknown or protein-based stains — blood, egg, milk — as heat sets them. Your reaction time matters more than the product you use.

sections.heading: Adapting the treatment to the stain
sections.body: Each type of stain has its own logic. Grease — oil, sauce — responds to an absorbent like talc or Fuller's Earth, then washing-up liquid. Fruit or wine stains respond to cold water quickly applied. Ink needs alcohol. Before applying anything to a visible area, test the product on a hidden part of the garment to check it doesn't bleach or damage the fabric.

sections.heading: When to hand it over
sections.body: If you're not sure what to do, or the piece is delicate — silk, wool, a valuable item — don't experiment with risky treatments. Just blot to absorb, avoid hot water, and take the piece to a dry cleaner and mention the stain. The cost of professional cleaning is trivial compared to losing a piece you love.

steps: Act as soon as the stain appears
Blot from edges to centre, don't rub
Avoid hot water on unknown stains
Adapt the treatment to the type of stain
Hand delicate or valuable pieces to a dry cleaner

faq.q: Should you rub a stain?
faq.a: No — rubbing spreads the stain and damages the fibres. Blot to absorb, working from the edges to the centre with a clean cloth.

faq.q: Does hot water help with stains?
faq.a: Not always: on unknown or protein-based stains, heat sets them. When in doubt, treat with cold water.

title: Wearing sneakers with any outfit
intro: Sneakers now work in almost any context. Well chosen, they even work with dressed-up outfits.

sections.heading: The minimal sneaker: the most versatile
sections.body: Not all sneakers are equal in versatility. A clean, minimal sneaker — leather or smooth canvas, in a solid colour, especially white or neutral — goes with almost everything, from jeans to smart trousers. Very technical, colourful, or chunky sneakers are more statement and harder to pair. If you could only have one go-everywhere pair, a clean understated sneaker would be the best choice.

sections.heading: Sneakers with dressed-up outfits
sections.body: Pairing sneakers with smarter pieces is now accepted — even stylish: a clean minimal white sneaker with suit trousers or a blazer creates a modern contrast between relaxed and polished. The key is that the sneaker is spotless and understated: an immaculate pair elevates the look; a tired pair drags it down. This combination works especially well in casual or creative contexts.

sections.heading: Cleanliness above all
sections.body: With sneakers more than any other shoe, cleanliness makes the style. A dirty or worn-out sneaker is noticed and ruins even the most carefully put-together outfit. Clean them regularly — as soon as a mark appears — and rotate between several pairs to let them breathe. A simple but spotless sneaker always looks better than an expensive but neglected one.

steps: Go for a clean, solid sneaker as your everyday pair
Don't be afraid to wear clean sneakers with a smart outfit
Keep your sneakers spotless at all times
Clean as soon as a mark appears
Rotate between several pairs

faq.q: Sneakers with a suit — does it work?
faq.a: Yes, in a casual or creative context, with a clean minimal white sneaker. In a very formal context, stick to classic shoes.

faq.q: Are all sneakers equally versatile?
faq.a: No. A clean, solid sneaker goes with everything; very technical, colourful, or chunky models are more statement and harder to pair.

title: Building a Wardrobe from Scratch
intro: First apartment, fresh start, major clear-out: building a wardrobe from zero is way simpler with a plan.

sections.heading: Start with basics
sections.body: When you're starting from nearly nothing, don't rush for trend pieces. Start with foundations: a few good neutral tops, one or two versatile bottoms, a jacket, a pair of go-with-everything shoes. These basics combine with each other and already give you several solid outfits. They're the base everything else gets layered onto later.

sections.heading: Build gradually
sections.body: No need to buy everything at once — that's expensive and you'll make mistakes. Spread purchases over several months. After the basics, observe what you actually need based on your real life: work, going out, sport. Then fill one gap at a time. This approach gives you time to understand your taste and adjust as you go. A wardrobe is cultivated, not bought in one haul.

sections.heading: Stick to a coherent palette
sections.body: From the start, lock in two or three neutral colours as your base, plus one or two accent colours you genuinely love. Keep that palette in mind when buying. The result: almost everything you own will mix by default, even if bought at different times. This simple discipline saves you from the classic trap of a packed wardrobe where nothing goes together.

steps: Buy basics first — things that combine with each other
Lock in a palette of 2–3 neutrals and 1–2 accents
Spread purchases over several months
Fill gaps based on your actual life, one at a time
Prioritise quality when the budget allows

faq.q: How many pieces do you need to start?
faq.a: About twenty well-chosen pieces is more than enough to build plenty of outfits. You add to it gradually based on your real needs.

faq.q: Should you buy everything at once?
faq.a: No — it's expensive and risky. Start with basics, then fill in progressively based on what you actually need, one gap at a time.

title: How Shoes Can Make or Break an Outfit
intro: Shoes set the tone for the whole look. Swap the pair and you can transform an outfit entirely.

sections.heading: Shoes define the register
sections.body: Shoes tell people how formal an outfit is. The same jeans and shirt read as casual with sneakers, put-together with derby shoes or ankle boots. They direct the whole interpretation of the look. When building an outfit, think about shoes early — they're not a final detail, they're an element that steers everything else.

sections.heading: Eyes always drop to the shoes
sections.body: The eye naturally travels downward and stops at the shoes. A great, well-maintained pair elevates the whole outfit; a beat-up pair tanks it instantly, no matter what you're wearing on top. That's why shoe care is a real style investment. Better to own a few impeccable pairs than a pile of tired ones that drag every look down.

sections.heading: Vary your pairs to vary your outfits
sections.body: Since shoes change the register, owning two or three different families — casual, dressed-up, and something in between — multiplies your outfit options from the same clothes. One shirt becomes three outfits depending on which pair you choose. Investing in shoe variety is often more efficient than accumulating similar tops or bottoms.

steps: Think about shoes early when building an outfit
Keep your pairs clean and properly maintained
Own at least one casual pair and one smarter pair
Use your shoes to vary the same base outfits
Invest in quality over quantity

faq.q: Where should your shoe budget go?
faq.a: Into quality and comfort for the pairs you wear most. One great versatile pair worn every day and lasting years is money well spent.

faq.q: Do shoes really change an outfit that much?
faq.a: Yes. They set the register: the same outfit reads casual in sneakers and polished in dress shoes.

title: Dressing Respectfully for a Funeral
intro: At a funeral, your outfit expresses respect and discretion. A few simple guidelines mean you never have to hesitate.

sections.heading: Sober and understated
sections.body: A funeral calls for a quiet, understated outfit. The goal isn't to stand out — it's to accompany others with dignity. Stick to dark, neutral colours: black, charcoal, navy. Classic, clean cuts. Avoid bright colours, loud patterns, overly casual or revealing clothing. Discretion is a form of respect towards the family and the person who has passed.

sections.heading: Respect cultural and family customs
sections.body: Customs vary by culture, religion, and family. In some traditions black is expected; in others, white or specific colours are appropriate. If you're unsure, ask someone close to the family — the question is entirely legitimate and well received. Some families express specific wishes, sometimes a particular colour in tribute. Following those wishes matters.

sections.heading: Practical and suitable for the setting
sections.body: A ceremony can move from indoors to outdoors, to the graveside, and can run for a while. Dress accordingly: a layer for the cold, stable shoes for walking on uneven ground. Being comfortable means you can stay fully present and focused on supporting the people around you, rather than being distracted by your clothes. Getting it right matters more than looking impressive.

steps: Choose dark, neutral colours
Go for classic, understated cuts
Check the customs of the family if you're unsure
Avoid bright colours and overly casual outfits
Bring a layer and wear stable shoes

faq.q: Is black always required?
faq.a: Not always. Black is standard but other sober colours work. Above all, respect the traditions and wishes of the family.

faq.q: Are bright colours always off-limits?
faq.a: Yes, unless the family specifically requests otherwise. Sober, dark tones are a mark of respect in most traditions.

title: Getting More Out of Denim Every Day
intro: Denim isn't just jeans. Jacket, shirt, full look — it's a versatile fabric that's surprisingly easy to wear.

sections.heading: A truly versatile fabric
sections.body: Denim is one of the most adaptable fabrics out there: tough, timeless, it works in every casual context and even some smarter ones. Beyond jeans, it comes in jackets, shirts, overshirts, sometimes shorts or dresses. Every denim piece combines easily with neutrals and even with other denim — as long as you follow a few simple principles.

sections.heading: Nailing the full-denim look
sections.body: Head-to-toe denim is absolutely doable and can look great, but it takes a little method. The rule: vary shades of blue rather than matching everything exactly. A lighter jacket over darker jeans — or the reverse — creates contrast and depth. Wearing the exact same shade of blue throughout looks uniform and a bit dated. Contrast in shade is the key.

sections.heading: Caring for denim
sections.body: Denim needs minimal care: wash it rarely, cold, inside out, to protect the colour. Raw, unwashed denim develops a patina over time and moulds to your body — that's its quality. Too many washes fade it and weaken the weave. Airing it out is often enough to freshen it up. Treated right, a good pair of jeans or a denim jacket can last you many years.

steps: Explore denim beyond jeans: jacket, shirt, overshirt
Pair denim with neutrals for easy combinations
For a full-denim look, vary the shades of blue
Wash denim rarely — cold, inside out
Let raw denim develop its patina over time

faq.q: Is head-to-toe denim a style mistake?
faq.a: No — as long as you vary the shades of blue between top and bottom. That contrast is what makes the look work and feel modern.

faq.q: How often should you wash denim?
faq.a: As rarely as possible. Wash cold, inside out. Frequent washing fades and weakens the fabric.

title: Staying Stylish in the Rain
intro: Rain doesn't mean sacrificing your look. With the right pieces, you can stay dry and pull it off.

sections.heading: The right waterproof layer
sections.body: The centrepiece of a rain outfit is the waterproof layer. A trench coat, a water-resistant parka, or a well-cut rain jacket protects you effectively while still looking good. Choose one in a neutral colour so it works with everything, and roomy enough to layer under. A good raincoat is an investment: it comes out every season and replaces the survival-plastic look of an emergency poncho.

sections.heading: Waterproof footwear
sections.body: Nothing ruins a day faster than soaked feet. In the rain, go for water-resistant shoes: treated leather, waterproofed ankle boots, or styles designed for wet conditions. A waterproofing spray applied regularly protects most leather or canvas shoes. Avoid absorbent materials and slippery soles. Dry feet genuinely change your whole day.

sections.heading: Useful accessories
sections.body: A few accessories complete a rain outfit. A sturdy, understated umbrella — not a flimsy one that turns inside out at the first gust. A well-designed hood on your raincoat frees your hands. For your bag, a resistant model or a rain cover protects your things. A bright colour or cheerful accessory can also cut through the gloom of a grey day.

steps: Invest in a well-cut, neutral-coloured raincoat
Choose water-resistant footwear
Regularly waterproof leather and canvas shoes
Carry a sturdy umbrella rather than a cheap one
Protect your bag and add a pop of colour

faq.q: How do you keep your feet dry?
faq.a: Wear water-resistant or well-waterproofed shoes, and avoid absorbent materials. A regular waterproofing spray makes a big difference.

faq.q: What makes a good raincoat?
faq.a: A well-cut model in a neutral colour, roomy enough for a layer underneath. A trench or water-resistant parka are both solid choices.

title: Expressing Your Personality Through What You Wear
intro: Style is a language: it tells people who you are before you say a word. It's a genuine form of self-expression.

sections.heading: Clothing as communication
sections.body: What you wear communicates something, whether you intend it to or not. Rather than letting that message happen by default, you can choose it. Style becomes a way to express your personality, values, mood, and world. That's not superficiality — it's a visual language, the same way you use words or decorate your space. You're allowed to use it.

sections.heading: Find your signatures
sections.body: Expressing your personality often works through signature elements: a colour you wear often, a recurring type of accessory, a recognisable piece. These signatures make you consistent and identifiable without any effort. They don't have to be spectacular — a small repeated constant is enough to define your style. Spot what you genuinely love and lean into it as your thing.

sections.heading: Stay free and let it evolve
sections.body: Expressing your personality doesn't mean locking yourself into a fixed image. Your personality has multiple sides and changes — your style can too. Some days you'll want simplicity, other days boldness, and that's healthy. What matters is that your choices come from you, not external pressure. The most expressive style is the one that honestly reflects who you are today.

steps: See your clothes as a form of expression
Identify what you genuinely love — not what's expected
Define 2–3 signatures: colour, accessory, or piece
Let your style shift with your mood
Check that your choices are actually coming from you

faq.q: What if my personality changes?
faq.a: That's normal and your style will follow. There's no need to lock yourself into a fixed image. Style evolving with you is actually a healthy sign.

faq.q: Is style really a language?
faq.a: Yes. Clothes communicate something whether you want them to or not. Choosing deliberately means deciding the message rather than just receiving one.

title: What to Wear on Your First Day at Work
intro: Your outfit on day one contributes to a solid first impression. The key: get it right without overdoing it.

sections.heading: When in doubt, go one notch up
sections.body: On day one, you don't yet know the real dress culture of the team. The safe rule: dress one notch above what you imagine the norm to be. It's much easier to relax afterwards than to recover from a first impression that was too casual. If you picked up any cues at your interview — what employees were wearing, the kind of office — use those to calibrate.

sections.heading: Clean, simple, and comfortable
sections.body: Your first day is packed with new information and emotions: your outfit should make life easier, not harder. Choose something neat, simple, and already tested — something you feel good in. Avoid brand new pieces you've never worn, which might feel awkward or surprise you. A polished, understated outfit frees your mind to focus on names, faces, and all the things you need to take in.

sections.heading: Observe and adjust from there
sections.body: Day one is also an observation exercise. Quietly notice how your colleagues dress at different levels of the team. From day two onwards, you can adjust your outfits to match the actual culture — loosen up a little or stay polished depending on what you see. That adaptation shows you read environments well, which is its own kind of skill. Day one sets the base; the days after refine it.

steps: Research the usual dress culture if you can beforehand
Dress one notch above what you assume the norm is
Choose a neat, simple, already-tested outfit
Avoid brand new pieces you've never worn before
Observe your colleagues and adjust from day two

faq.q: What if I'm overdressed on day one?
faq.a: Not a problem — it's better than the alternative. You adjust the next day. Being too casual leaves an impression that's harder to correct.

faq.q: How do you know what to wear on day one?
faq.a: Use cues from the interview, and when in doubt, go one notch up. You'll calibrate by watching your colleagues from day two.

title: Wearing White and Keeping It Immaculate
intro: White is luminous and elegant — but it shows everything. A few habits let you wear it without stress.

sections.heading: Why white works
sections.body: White brings freshness and brightness to an outfit. It lights up your complexion, gives a clean, summery feel, and pairs with absolutely every colour. A well-cut white top is a genuinely valuable basic. Its only challenge is care: white shows every stain and greys over time if washed wrong. Managed well, it's not a problem.

sections.heading: Washing and brightening white
sections.body: To keep white bright, wash it only with other whites — never with colours that might bleed. Don't overdo the detergent; excess can actually yellow the fibres. If white has turned grey, products designed for white laundry or a gentle soak with sodium percarbonate can restore the brightness. Drying in sunlight also naturally whitens fabric.

sections.heading: Wearing it without anxiety
sections.body: Rather than avoiding white for fear of stains, learn to wear it calmly. Keep a stain remover to hand so you can act immediately — a stain treated right away almost always comes out. You might save your whitest pieces for lower-risk situations and pull them out when you want that bright, clean effect. Wear white with pleasure, not anxiety.

steps: Embrace white for the brightness it brings
Wash whites separately, away from colours
Don't overdose the detergent — it can cause yellowing
Restore greyed white with a dedicated whitening product
Keep a stain remover accessible and act fast

faq.q: How do you rescue grey-looking white?
faq.a: A soak with a white laundry product or sodium percarbonate, followed by drying in sunlight, usually brings the brightness back.

faq.q: Does white need special care?
faq.a: Wash it separately from colours and don't overload the detergent. Sun-drying also helps maintain whiteness naturally.

title: Knowing When to Spend More on a Piece
intro: Not every piece deserves the same budget. Knowing where to invest makes every euro work harder.

sections.heading: The rule: frequency and visibility
sections.body: Two criteria decide where your budget goes: how often you wear the piece, and how visible it is. Something you wear constantly and that's highly visible — coat, shoes, jeans — deserves investment. It gets a lot of use and sets the tone for your outfits. A piece you wear rarely or that's barely noticeable can stay inexpensive. This simple logic puts money where it has the most impact.

sections.heading: Spotting real quality
sections.body: Quality isn't about price or brand. Look at the fabric — natural composition, decent weight — the stitching — regular and tight — and the finishing: clean lining, buttons firmly attached, smooth zips. A quality piece holds its shape, ages well, and can be repaired. Learning to spot these signs means you can find genuine quality at lower prices, especially secondhand.

sections.heading: True cost over time
sections.body: A quality piece looks expensive upfront, but its real cost is calculated over time. A solid coat worn a hundred times over five years costs very little per wear; a cheap piece that falls apart in one season ends up costing more. That's the cost-per-wear principle. Investing in durable things is usually more economical than constantly replacing disposable ones.

steps: Invest in what you wear often and what shows
Save on pieces you wear rarely or that stay hidden
Check fabric, stitching, and finishing quality
Calculate cost-per-wear, not purchase price
Look for quality secondhand too

faq.q: Does expensive mean quality?
faq.a: Not necessarily. Price also reflects branding and marketing. Trust the fabric, stitching, and finishing to judge real quality.

faq.q: Where should your budget go?
faq.a: Into what you wear most and what's most visible: coat, shoes, jeans. Save on pieces you wear rarely or that barely show.

title: Wearing Floral Prints with Confidence
intro: Floral prints bring freshness and personality. Anyone can wear them, in any season.

sections.heading: A pattern for everyone
sections.body: Floral prints have no gender and no reserved season. A floral shirt, a printed top, or a flowery accessory is for anyone. Depending on the scale of the print and the colours, the effect ranges from subtle to bold. Small pattern on a dark base — understated. Large colourful flowers — it becomes a statement piece. Choose based on your comfort level and the effect you want.

sections.heading: Balance with simplicity
sections.body: A floral print is already visually rich: to make it work, keep the rest of the outfit simple. A floral piece over neutral, plain bottoms and shoes lets the print breathe and become the focal point. Avoid pairing a floral with another loud pattern. The principle is always the same: one point of attention, everything else in support.

sections.heading: Choosing your print
sections.body: For an easy first floral, look for a neutral base — dark or light — with flowers in a limited palette of two or three colours. Prints that are too multicoloured or too dense are harder to style. Also check the dominant colours of the print: they should work with your usual palette so the piece integrates naturally into the rest of your wardrobe.

steps: Choose print scale based on the effect you want
Keep the rest of the outfit plain and neutral
Don't combine two loud patterns
Start with a print in a limited colour palette
Make sure the colours work with your wardrobe

faq.q: Are florals only for spring?
faq.a: No. Dark or deep-toned florals work brilliantly in autumn and winter. It all depends on the colours of the print.

faq.q: Are floral prints gendered?
faq.a: No. Florals are for anyone. It's the scale and colours that change the effect — not the gender.

title: Dressing for an Important Professional Meeting
intro: Key meeting, client pitch, negotiation: your outfit supports your credibility when it counts.

sections.heading: Your outfit sets the tone for trust
sections.body: In an important professional meeting, your counterpart forms a quick first impression. A polished, appropriate outfit signals seriousness and reliability before a word is spoken. That doesn't mean flashy — clean, well-matched, one notch above casual is enough to show you take the meeting and the person seriously.

sections.heading: Adapt to who you're meeting
sections.body: The right formality depends on who you're meeting and where. A meeting with a client in a formal industry calls for more rigour than a chat with a creative startup. Research the world your counterpart operates in. The goal is to be in sync with them — not too formal, not too casual. That alignment shows you understand their world.

sections.heading: Be comfortable enough to be convincing
sections.body: An important meeting needs your full concentration and ease. An uncomfortable outfit — something that pinches, scratches, or that you've never worn before — is a distraction. Choose tested pieces you feel solid in. Prepare your outfit in advance and make sure it's impeccable. Feeling well dressed boosts your confidence, and confidence is convincing.

steps: Research your counterpart's industry and culture
Aim for a clean, polished, one-notch-up outfit
Adapt the formality level to the context
Choose pieces you've worn before and feel good in
Prepare and check your outfit in advance

faq.q: Better to be overdressed or underdressed?
faq.a: When in doubt, go one notch up. Looking invested is easier to recover from than looking slightly careless. But stay in line with their sector.

faq.q: How do you adapt your outfit to the person you're meeting?
faq.a: Research their world and sector, then aim for an outfit that's in sync with theirs — not too formal, not too relaxed.

title: Choosing Glasses That Work With Your Look
intro: Prescription glasses or sunglasses — frames are always-on accessories. Chosen well, they elevate your whole face.

sections.heading: Frames are a full-blown accessory
sections.body: Glasses sit at the centre of your face: they're one of the most visible accessories you own. The right frames can become a style signature — something that makes you recognisable. Unlike accessories you take on and off, prescription glasses are on you all day. It's worth choosing them carefully, as a genuine element of your image.

sections.heading: Find frames that feel like you
sections.body: You often hear that frame shape should match face shape. That's a useful reference, but not an absolute rule. What matters most is trying on multiple frames and keeping the ones you recognise yourself in and feel good wearing. Look at how they balance with your face, and how the colour works with your skin tone and hair. Trust your gut as much as the advice.

sections.heading: Practical and long-lasting
sections.body: Beyond style, frames need to be comfortable and suited to your life. Check they stay on properly, don't slide, and don't press on your temples. For everyday wear, a solid, versatile frame in a neutral colour will go with all your outfits. If the budget allows, a second pair in a different style lets you mix it up. Take care of them — case, regular cleaning — and they'll last.

steps: Treat frames as a key accessory, not an afterthought
Try on multiple shapes and keep what feels like you
Check how the colour works with your skin and hair
Make sure they're comfortable and well-fitted
Take care of them so they last

faq.q: Do you have to match frames to your face shape?
faq.a: It's a useful guide but not a rule. What matters most is choosing frames you recognise yourself in and feel comfortable wearing.

faq.q: How many pairs of glasses do you need?
faq.a: One versatile pair is enough. If the budget allows, a second pair in a different style lets you switch up your look.

title: Wearing Hats and Caps Like You Mean It
intro: A well-chosen hat finishes an outfit and protects from the sun. The trick is wearing it with ease, not hesitation.

sections.heading: A piece that transforms the look
sections.body: Cap, beanie, hat, bucket hat: a headpiece draws the eye upward and completes an outfit. It can become a genuine style signature. Beyond aesthetics, it's practical: sun protection in summer, warmth in winter. Lots of people avoid hats out of self-consciousness, when a headpiece worn with confidence is simply another accessory.

sections.heading: Choose the right style
sections.body: Each headpiece has its register: caps are casual and sporty, beanies are wintery and cosy, hats skew smarter or bohemian, bucket hats are summery and relaxed. Choose based on the tone of your outfit and the occasion. On sizing, a headpiece should stay on without squeezing or floating. To start, a simple model in a neutral colour works with almost everything you own.

sections.heading: Wear it with ease
sections.body: The secret to a hat that works is confidence: worn with hesitation it looks like a costume; worn naturally it just looks like you. To get comfortable with it, start in relaxed settings until it feels like part of you. Avoid overloading: if your hat is a strong piece, keep the rest simple. Treat a headpiece as a focal point, not an afterthought.

steps: Choose a style that matches the tone of your outfit
Check the fit: neither too tight nor too loose
Start with a simple, neutral model
Wear it first in relaxed, low-stakes contexts
Keep the rest of the outfit simple

faq.q: How do you build the confidence to wear a hat?
faq.a: Start in casual settings and keep it on until it feels natural. Confidence does all the work: worn with ease, a hat is just another accessory.

faq.q: What headpiece to start with?
faq.a: A simple model in a neutral colour suited to your outfits: a cap for casual looks, a beanie for winter.

title: Selling Your Clothes Secondhand
intro: Selling what you no longer wear clears space and funds your next purchases. A few habits boost your chances of a sale.

sections.heading: Why bother reselling
sections.body: Reselling clothes you no longer wear has several advantages: you get a bit of money back, you free up space, and you extend the life of pieces instead of leaving them unused. It's also an ecological choice. Sorting through things to resell is a great occasion to clarify your wardrobe. Something you haven't worn in a year has more value in someone else's hands than in your closet.

sections.heading: Prepare your pieces properly
sections.body: A clean, well-presented piece sells better and for more. Wash and iron the item before listing it. Take clear photos in natural daylight, on a neutral background, showing the piece from multiple angles and honestly showing any flaws. Note the brand, size, fabric, and real measurements. A tidy listing inspires trust and gets you to a sale faster.

sections.heading: Price it right
sections.body: To set your price, search for similar pieces on the same platform. Factor in condition, brand, and age. Pricing too high puts buyers off; pricing too low undersells you. Be realistic: secondhand typically sells well below retail. Stay responsive to questions from buyers and open to a little negotiation.

steps: Sort out what you haven't worn in a year
Wash and iron pieces before listing
Photograph in daylight, multiple angles, show any flaws
Note brand, size, fabric, and real measurements
Set a realistic price by comparing similar listings

faq.q: What sells best secondhand?
faq.a: Branded pieces in good condition that are in demand and in season. But plenty of ordinary clothes find buyers when they're clean and well presented.

faq.q: How do you present a piece well for sale?
faq.a: Wash and iron it, photograph it in natural daylight from several angles, and honestly describe the brand, size, and any flaws.

title: Mastering Neutrals and Tonal Beige Outfits
intro: Neutral tones create quietly elegant outfits. A tonal beige look is a classic that's easier to pull off than you think.

sections.heading: The strength of neutrals
sections.body: Neutral tones — beige, ecru, camel, taupe, grey, off-white — give a calm, refined look. They combine with each other effortlessly and create harmonious outfits that never feel loud. A wardrobe built on neutrals has immediate coherence: almost everything goes together. It's an ideal foundation — understated, but far from boring when you play it well.

sections.heading: Nailing a tonal look
sections.body: A tonal or tone-on-tone outfit means wearing several close shades from the same family. A tonal beige — from light cream to deep camel or brown — creates a chic, calming look. The secret to avoiding a flat effect: vary the textures. Pairing a cotton, a knitwear piece, and a leather item in similar beige tones creates dimension through the way each fabric catches light differently.

sections.heading: Adding a spark to neutrals
sections.body: An all-neutral outfit can, if you want, take a touch of contrast: a coloured accessory, a bright white piece, or something dark like black. It energises without breaking the elegance. But neutrals work brilliantly on their own too. Also lean into details and clean finishes — on a spare palette, they stand out and make all the difference.

steps: Build a base of versatile neutral tones
For a tonal look, choose close shades from the same family
Vary textures to create dimension
Add a contrast element if you want a little spark
Polish the details — they stand out on a neutral base

faq.q: Isn't an all-beige outfit boring?
faq.a: Not if you play with textures and shades. A well-composed tonal beige outfit is elegant and anything but dull.

faq.q: Are neutrals really not boring?
faq.a: No. A well-played neutral palette — varied shades and fabrics — is elegant and far from monotonous.

title: Dressing Well Even When You Hate Clothes Shopping
intro: Not everyone cares about fashion, and that's completely fine. You can look put-together without spending any mental energy on it.

sections.heading: You don't have to love fashion
sections.body: Whether or not you're into fashion is a personal preference — no judgement. If the whole subject bores you, the goal isn't to become passionate about it. It's to have a simple system that keeps you presentable without thinking about it. Style can be reduced to an efficient routine. Plenty of well-dressed people aren't particularly into clothes — they just have a good system.

sections.heading: The personal uniform
sections.body: The most effective solution for people who hate getting dressed is a personal uniform: find one or two outfit formulas that work, then repeat them. For example, always the same type of jeans, top, and shoes in a few neutral colours. Buy multiples of what works, and every morning the decision disappears. Simple, reliable, zero mental effort.

sections.heading: The minimum that's enough
sections.body: Even with no interest in style, a few basics guarantee you look decent: clean clothes, the right fit, good condition, colours that go together. That's it. No need to follow trends or constantly vary. A small number of good neutral pieces, well maintained, covers almost every situation without ever spending mental energy on it.

steps: Accept that fashion doesn't interest you — no guilt
Define 1–2 outfit formulas that actually work
Buy multiples of what works
Stick to neutral colours that mix easily
Keep your clothes clean and in good condition

faq.q: Can you look good without being into fashion?
faq.a: Completely. A simple personal uniform and some well-maintained neutral pieces will keep you looking presentable without ever thinking about it.

faq.q: What's the simplest approach?
faq.a: A personal uniform: one or two outfit formulas that work, bought in multiples. The morning decision disappears entirely.

title: What to Wear to Your Graduation
intro: Graduation marks a real milestone. Your outfit should be polished, a little festive, and comfortable enough for the whole day.

sections.heading: Mark the occasion
sections.body: Graduation is a celebration — your outfit can reflect that. Go for something polished and slightly festive, a notch above daily wear, without going overly formal unless the event requires it. It's also a heavily photographed day: pick something you'll be happy to look back on. A personal touch — a colour you love — makes the photos more authentically you.

sections.heading: Factor in the gown
sections.body: At most graduation ceremonies, you'll wear a gown over your outfit. Account for that: the gown covers most of your torso, so the eye falls on your lower half — legs and shoes — and on the collar visible above. Choose clean, well-maintained shoes and a neat bottom half. Also avoid anything too thick underneath: a packed hall and a gown on top gets warm fast.

sections.heading: Comfort for a long day
sections.body: A graduation ceremony stretches: waiting, speeches, the ceremony itself, photos, often a celebration after. Choose an outfit — and especially shoes — you can stand and sit in for hours. You might step up onto a stage: stable shoes matter. Being comfortable means you can actually enjoy the moment you've worked for, without being distracted by what you're wearing.

steps: Go for polished and a little festive
Choose something that will photograph well
Pay attention to the lower half and shoes, visible under the gown
Avoid thick layers under the gown
Pick stable, comfortable shoes

faq.q: What's visible if I'm wearing a gown?
faq.a: Mainly the bottom half, your shoes, and your collar above the gown. Focus your attention there and wear something light underneath.

faq.q: Should I plan for being warm under the gown?
faq.a: Yes. A full hall and a gown on top heats up quickly — wear light layers underneath to stay comfortable.

title: Letting Your Style Evolve as You Grow
intro: Style travels with your life and changes with it. Letting it evolve gradually, without pressure, is part of the pleasure.

sections.heading: Style isn't static
sections.body: Your tastes, lifestyle, and desires shift over the years — it's normal and healthy for your style to follow. What you wore at seventeen doesn't have to look the same at twenty-five or thirty. Evolution isn't a betrayal; it's adaptation to who you're becoming. No need to cling to a past image, or to flip everything overnight because of outside pressure.

sections.heading: Evolve without age pressure
sections.body: You hear a lot of rules about what you should or shouldn't wear at a certain age. Take them with a grain of salt: there's no expiration date on a colour or a cut you love. Style is chosen based on your taste and comfort, not a countdown. Evolution should come from your genuine desires, not from someone else's idea of what's appropriate for your age.

sections.heading: Small steps, not revolutions
sections.body: Evolving your style happens through small touches, not overhauls. Gradually bring in pieces that reflect who you are now; gently let go of things that no longer speak to you. Keep what you still love, even if it's from years ago. This gradual shift stays coherent and economical. Your style matures naturally, alongside you — no dramatic break, no full wardrobe replacement required.

steps: Accept that your style will change — that's healthy
Ignore rules about what to wear at a certain age
Let evolution come from your genuine desires
Add and remove pieces gradually, one at a time
Keep what you still love, regardless of when you got it

faq.q: Is there an age to stop wearing certain things?
faq.a: No. No colour or cut has an expiry date. If a piece makes you happy and feels right, you can wear it at any age.

faq.q: Is changing your style betraying your past?
faq.a: Not at all. It's a healthy adaptation to who you're becoming. You can keep what you still love and let the rest evolve naturally.

title: How to Wear Stripes Without Getting It Wrong
intro: Stripes are a graphic, timeless pattern. Breton, thin lines, wide bands — each stripe has its own effect.

sections.heading: A timeless classic
sections.body: Stripes have never gone out of fashion. The Breton — blue and white — is an easy, elegant classic. Stripes come in infinite variations: thin or wide, high-contrast or subtle, horizontal or vertical. It's a graphic pattern that adds structure to an outfit while staying understated. Chosen well, stripes are one of the easiest patterns to wear.

sections.heading: Effect by stripe type
sections.body: Different stripes produce different effects. Thin, close-together stripes are subtle and versatile. Wide stripes are bolder and make more of a statement. Vertical stripes create an elongating effect; horizontal ones can widen. Contrast matters too: navy and white is sharp and graphic, close tones give a softer result. Pick based on the effect you're after.

sections.heading: Pairing stripes
sections.body: A striped piece works easily over plain, neutral bottoms and accessories — the stripe becomes the graphic focal point of the outfit. Mixing stripes with another pattern is possible but advanced: you need to vary the scales and stick to a shared palette. To keep it simple, wear only one striped piece per outfit. Stripes pair especially well with a neutral base.

steps: Choose stripe width based on the effect you want
Think about direction: vertical elongates, horizontal widens
Wear a striped piece over plain neutral basics
Stick to one striped piece per outfit to start
Pair stripes with neutrals for an easy result

faq.q: Do horizontal stripes really make you look wider?
faq.a: The effect exists but is subtle and depends on contrast and cut. Don't skip a Breton stripe for that reason — wear what you like.

faq.q: Is the Breton stripe timeless?
faq.a: Yes — it's a true classic. Blue and white, easy and chic, it works year-round with almost anything.

title: Packing a Stylish, Practical Suitcase
intro: Packing well means taking little but having plenty of outfits to build. A mini travelling wardrobe.

sections.heading: Think in outfits, not in pieces
sections.body: The classic packing mistake is throwing clothes in at random, then realising on arrival that nothing goes together. The right approach: think in complete outfits. Plan exactly what you'll wear each day based on your schedule and the weather. Better still, choose pieces that all combine with each other, so you can multiply outfit options from a small number of items.

sections.heading: The travel capsule
sections.body: Apply the capsule wardrobe principle to your suitcase: a limited colour palette, versatile pieces that all mix together. A few tops, two bottoms, and a jacket in coordinating tones already give you a large number of combinations. Add one slightly smarter piece for an evening out, and shoes chosen for the activities planned — never in excess.

sections.heading: Optimise the packing
sections.body: To save space and limit creases, roll soft clothes rather than folding them flat. Packing cubes help organise and compress. Put shoes in bags, soles against the sides of the case. Keep a change of clothes and the essentials in your carry-on in case of delays. Travelling light makes the whole trip more enjoyable.

steps: Plan your outfits based on your schedule and weather
Choose a limited, coordinated colour palette
Pick pieces that all mix with each other
Roll soft clothes to save space
Keep a backup outfit in your carry-on

faq.q: How do you avoid over-packing?
faq.a: Plan every outfit in advance and choose versatile pieces that combine. You take less and end up with more options.

faq.q: Roll or fold clothes in a suitcase?
faq.a: Roll soft pieces to save space and reduce creases. Packing cubes also help organise and compress things.

title: Wearing Pastel Colours Well
intro: Pastels bring freshness and softness to any outfit. Easy to combine, they work for everyone.

sections.heading: The charm of pastels
sections.body: Pastel colours — powder pink, sky blue, mint green, lavender, pale yellow — are softened, luminous tones that aren't aggressive. They give a gentle, soothing feel and work in all seasons, especially spring. Easier to wear than vivid colours because they're less intense, they're a great entry point for anyone looking to move beyond all-neutrals.

sections.heading: Pairing pastels
sections.body: Pastels work beautifully together: multiple pastels in one outfit create a soft, harmonious look with no risk of overload, since the tones are low-intensity. They also pair perfectly with neutrals — white, beige, grey — which let them shine. For a sharper contrast, a pastel pops nicely against denim or something dark. Pastels are among the easiest colours to combine.

sections.heading: Pastels for everyone
sections.body: Pastels have no reserved gender or season — they're open to everyone. To wear them with confidence, find the shades that suit your complexion: some pastels run warm, others cool. Try them near your face to see the effect. If you're new to pastels, start with one piece and build from there. Even one pastel touch softens and brightens an entire outfit.

steps: Think of pastels as an easy colour option
Combine multiple pastels for a soft look
Pair them with neutrals or denim
Find the shades that work with your complexion
Start with one piece and build confidence from there

faq.q: Are pastels only for spring?
faq.a: No. They're perfect in spring but work year-round. In winter, a pastel actually adds a lovely touch of light.

faq.q: Are pastels easy to combine?
faq.a: Yes — among the easiest. They work together, with neutrals, and with denim, all without risk of overload.

title: Taking Care of Your Overall Image Every Day
intro: The image you project goes beyond your clothes. Posture, grooming, consistency — everything contributes to a confident presence.

sections.heading: Image is a coherent whole
sections.body: Taking care of your image isn't just about clothes — it's a package. General cleanliness, neat hair, clean nails, well-maintained clothing, and your posture all count as much as the outfit itself. A great outfit worn with a hunched back and a tired expression loses its impact. Conversely, a simple outfit worn with confidence and care reads as genuinely positive and solid.

sections.heading: Posture is a major detail
sections.body: Posture transforms how an outfit is perceived and how you're perceived. Standing tall, shoulders relaxed but open, head up — it immediately adds presence and confidence without buying a thing. It's free and powerful. Posture improves through awareness: think about it regularly throughout the day, while walking, sitting, until straightening up becomes natural.

sections.heading: Consistency and being yourself
sections.body: A strong image is above all consistent: outfit, attitude, and personality aligned. No need to overdo it or play a character — naturalness and sincerity register with people and put them at ease. Taking care of your image is about presenting yourself at your best while still being you. The goal isn't to impress — it's to feel aligned, which naturally translates into an appealing presence.

steps: Take care of your general cleanliness — not just your outfit
Work on your posture: back straight, head up
Think about straightening up several times a day
Aim for consistency between your outfit and your attitude
Stay natural — don't play a role

faq.q: What matters most in terms of image?
faq.a: Consistency and attitude. A polished outfit worn with good posture and genuine ease has more impact than a spectacular look you're not comfortable in.

faq.q: Does posture really matter that much?
faq.a: Enormously. Standing straight, head up, gives you more presence and confidence than any piece of clothing — and it's completely free.

title: Choosing a Bag That's Both Practical and Stylish
intro: Your bag is an accessory you carry almost every day. Chosen well, it combines utility and style for the long haul.

sections.heading: Start from actual use
sections.body: Before thinking about style, think about function: what do you actually carry day to day? A large bag for a laptop and school or work stuff, a medium bag for going out, a small bag for the essentials. Choosing a bag that fits your real needs prevents frustration and regret. A bag too small irritates you; a bag too big just weighs you down. Start from your actual life, not from a wishlist.

sections.heading: A versatile, durable bag
sections.body: As with clothes, a bag in a neutral colour — black, brown, beige — will go with most of your outfits and serve you for years. Prioritise a classic shape over a very trend-specific model that will date fast. Check for durability: stitching, handles, fastenings, base. A good bag is an investment that can last years, especially in leather if well cared for.

sections.heading: The bag as a style element
sections.body: Beyond utility, your bag contributes to the outfit. It can echo your shoes or belt in colour — without matching perfectly. It can also bring the pop of colour or character to an otherwise understated outfit. If you own multiple bags, varying them by occasion — casual or dressed-up — completes your looks nicely. Take care of yours so it stays looking fresh.

steps: Identify what you actually carry every day
Choose a size suited to that real use
Go for a neutral colour and a classic shape
Check stitching and handles for durability
Let the bag echo your shoes and belt in tone

faq.q: Should your bag match your shoes?
faq.a: Not exactly. A colour or texture echo is enough and looks more elegant. Matching everything perfectly can actually look stiff.

faq.q: What colour bag should you choose?
faq.a: A neutral — black, brown, or beige — works with most of your outfits and stays versatile over time.

title: Keeping Your Clothes Fresh and Odour-Free
intro: Clothes that smell fresh are part of a polished image. A few simple habits keep your wardrobe smelling clean.

sections.heading: Airing: the basic habit
sections.body: A worn piece doesn't always need washing — often it just needs air. Hanging something up after you've worn it, in a ventilated spot, removes moisture and light odours. Avoid immediately putting worn clothes back in the wardrobe or piling them in a hamper: trapped moisture breeds bad smells. Airing also extends the life of your clothes.

sections.heading: Wash and dry properly
sections.body: Persistent odours often come from a badly done wash. Don't overload the machine — clothes need room to rinse properly. Don't leave wet laundry sitting in the drum: it quickly picks up a musty smell. Always dry completely before putting things away; storing damp clothes causes mould. For synthetic fabrics that hold odours, a thorough wash is essential.

sections.heading: Keep the wardrobe healthy
sections.body: A clean, dry wardrobe keeps clothes fresh. Air it out occasionally, don't overpack it — crammed clothes can't breathe. Store only perfectly dry, clean pieces. You can slip in a natural scented sachet or cedar, which smells good and deters moths. Avoid excessive air fresheners: a healthy wardrobe simply smells clean, which is already pleasant.

steps: Air worn clothes before putting them away
Don't overload the washing machine
Take laundry out of the drum as soon as the cycle ends
Dry completely before storing
Keep the wardrobe aired and not overpacked

faq.q: Why does my laundry smell bad even after washing?
faq.a: Usually because it sat damp in the machine too long, or the drum was overfilled. Get laundry out quickly and give it room.

faq.q: Do you need to wash a piece after every wear?
faq.a: Not always. Many pieces just need to be aired. Airing is often enough to freshen something that's barely been soiled.

title: Nailing a Costume Party Outfit
intro: A themed party is an opportunity to have fun. With a bit of planning, a great costume doesn't need a big budget.

sections.heading: Plan ahead and choose your idea
sections.body: The key to a great costume is planning: leaving it until the night before usually ends in disappointment. As soon as you know the theme, start thinking. Pick a character or concept you genuinely want to embody and that's actually achievable. A simple idea executed well beats an ambitious idea half-done. Make sure the costume will be comfortable enough for a whole evening.

sections.heading: Build it without buying everything
sections.body: A good costume doesn't require a complete purchased outfit. Start in your own wardrobe: lots of characters can be built from clothes you already own plus one or two key accessories. Those accessories — chosen well — are what make a character recognisable. Secondhand shops, borrowing from friends, and a bit of creativity deliver a great costume on a tight budget.

sections.heading: The details that make the character
sections.body: What makes a costume convincing is usually a few well-identified details: a hairstyle, a signature colour, an iconic accessory, an attitude. Focus your effort on those recognisable elements rather than chasing general perfection. A costume worn with humour and confidence always lands better than a technically perfect outfit worn with awkwardness. The spirit of the party comes first.

steps: Start thinking about your idea as soon as the theme is announced
Choose a concept that's achievable and comfortable
Dig through your existing wardrobe first
Focus on 2–3 recognisable accessories
Wear it with humour and confidence

faq.q: How do you pull off a costume without spending much?
faq.a: Use clothes you already own and add a few key accessories. Borrowing from friends and secondhand complete the picture. Details matter more than budget.

faq.q: Do you have to buy a full costume?
faq.a: No. Most characters can be built from what you already have plus a couple of key accessories. Details count more than budget.

title: Wearing Jewellery With Taste
intro: Jewellery personalises an outfit and tells people something about you. Well-dosed, it elevates without overwhelming.

sections.heading: A personal touch
sections.body: Rings, necklaces, bracelets, earrings — jewellery is a subtle but powerful way to assert your personality. A piece can become a recognisable signature: a ring you always wear, a go-to necklace. There are no gender rules or strict guidelines — wear what you love. A piece can also hold sentimental value, which makes it all the more worth wearing.

sections.heading: Dose it and harmonise
sections.body: The dosing principle applies to jewellery too: a few chosen pieces beat an accumulation. You can layer — several fine necklaces or bracelets together, for example — but keep it coherent. For metals, staying within the same family, gold or silver, gives a harmonious result; mixing is possible but more advanced. If you're wearing one bold piece, keep the rest light.

sections.heading: Match the outfit and the occasion
sections.body: Choose jewellery in line with your outfit: subtle pieces for a look that's already busy, bolder jewellery to bring life to a simple outfit. Also think about context: fine, discreet pieces work everywhere; imposing ones suit evenings out better. On maintenance: store jewellery safely and clean it occasionally — pieces will stay bright and last longer.

steps: Choose jewellery that genuinely resonates with you
Go for a few chosen pieces rather than accumulation
Keep metal families consistent for harmony
Dial back the rest when wearing a bold statement piece
Store and clean your jewellery to make it last

faq.q: Can you mix gold and silver?
faq.a: Yes, it's actually on trend — but it's more advanced. For a safe result, start by sticking to one metal family.

faq.q: How much jewellery to wear at once?
faq.a: A few chosen pieces rather than a pile-up. You can layer fine pieces, but keep it light if you're wearing something bold.

title: Dressing for a Customer-Facing Student Job
intro: A job in retail, a café, or reception needs a clean, practical outfit. The key: neat, understated, and suited to the place.

sections.heading: Follow the employer's guidelines
sections.body: Many customer-facing jobs have a uniform or specific dress requirements: approved colours, level of presentation, closed-toe shoes. Find out from day one and follow those rules — they're part of the job. If there's no uniform, watch what colleagues wear. Your outfit represents the business to customers, which is why following the guidelines matters.

sections.heading: Practical and comfortable
sections.body: A service job means being on your feet, moving around, sometimes for hours on end. Your outfit needs to follow: clothes you can move freely in, bend in, carry things in — and especially closed-toe, comfortable, already broken-in shoes. Avoid anything that's too tight or that you'd be worried about getting dirty. A practical outfit helps you last the shift without discomfort.

sections.heading: Neat and understated
sections.body: When dealing with the public, a clean, polished outfit builds confidence. Go for simplicity: neutral colours, minimal loud patterns, clothes in good condition. Avoid anything too casual or overly statement-making. The goal is to look professional and approachable. A tidy appearance — clean clothes, neat hair — makes a good impression on both customers and your employer.

steps: Find out about the uniform or dress code from day one
Follow the establishment's guidelines
Choose closed-toe, comfortable shoes you've already worn
Pick a practical, easy-to-maintain outfit
Aim for clean, understated, and presentable

faq.q: What if no outfit is required?
faq.a: Watch your colleagues and aim for neat and understated: neutral colours, clean and practical clothes, closed-toe shoes. You represent the business.

faq.q: What shoes for a service job?
faq.a: Closed-toe and comfortable, already broken in. You'll be on your feet and moving for hours at a time.

title: Decoding Dress Codes on Invitations
intro: Casual, smart casual, cocktail, black tie: these terms on an invitation mean something specific. Knowing them prevents an embarrassing misstep.

sections.heading: Why dress codes exist
sections.body: A dress code on an invitation isn't an arbitrary constraint — it helps guests know what to wear and creates a sense of coherence. Respecting it is a courtesy to your hosts. Showing up wildly off-register — too dressed up or too casual — creates unnecessary awkwardness. Understanding the main codes means you can dress appropriately without stress.

sections.heading: The common codes
sections.body: Casual means relaxed but presentable — no evening wear, but nothing sloppy either. Smart casual or 'business casual' steps it up: clean trousers, a dressed shirt or smart top, possibly a jacket. Cocktail means elegant evening wear without going ultra-formal. Black tie or formal means the most dressed-up level. When in doubt, place the code on that scale.

sections.heading: Handling uncertainty
sections.body: If the code is vague or missing, two instincts: ask the host or another guest — the question is entirely normal — and factor in the venue and time. A dinner in a fine restaurant on a Saturday evening calls for more care than a Sunday brunch. If still uncertain, err on the side of slightly overdressed rather than underdressed: it's easier to tone down than to recover from too casual.

steps: Spot the dress code on the invitation
Place it on the scale from relaxed to very formal
Factor in the venue and time of day
Ask the host if the code is still unclear
When in doubt, go one notch up

faq.q: What does smart casual actually mean?
faq.a: A middle ground: more polished than everyday, less formal than evening wear. Clean trousers, a smart top, possibly a jacket.

faq.q: What if there's no dress code listed?
faq.a: Factor in the venue and time, and check with the host if needed. When in doubt, go slightly more dressed than you think.

title: Wearing Leather and Faux Leather with Style
intro: Leather — real or synthetic — brings character to an outfit. Jacket, accessories: it's a strong, lasting fabric.

sections.heading: A fabric with presence
sections.body: Leather and its alternative, faux leather, have a distinct visual presence: the material catches light and adds texture. A leather jacket — biker or otherwise — structures an outfit and adds instant character. Leather also appears in accessories — belt, bag, shoes — that bring that same edge. It's a material that gets noticed: often one piece is enough.

sections.heading: Balance a leather piece
sections.body: Like any strong piece, leather works best on a simpler base so it doesn't overwhelm. A leather jacket over a plain top and jeans is a safe bet. Don't stack multiple large leather pieces. Leather contrasts beautifully with softer fabrics — knitwear, fluid cotton: that hard-soft texture mix creates an interesting, balanced look.

sections.heading: Caring for leather
sections.body: Real leather lasts a very long time with proper care: condition it occasionally with a suitable product to prevent it drying out and cracking, protect it from heavy rain and direct heat. Faux leather is more economical and animal-free, needs less maintenance, but generally ages less gracefully. In both cases, store leather pieces flat or on a hanger, away from light.

steps: Treat leather as a strong, statement piece
Wear it over a simple base
Don't stack multiple large leather pieces
Play the contrast: leather against soft fabrics
Condition and protect real leather regularly

faq.q: Real leather or faux leather?
faq.a: Real leather lasts longer and develops a patina, but costs more. Faux leather is affordable and animal-free. Choose based on your values and budget.

faq.q: How do you avoid overloading an outfit with leather?
faq.a: Wear one large leather piece on a simple base, and contrast it with soft fabrics like knitwear or cotton.

title: Dressing Well Even When You're Staying In
intro: Being at home doesn't mean giving up on feeling well dressed. Comfort and a minimum of care can coexist.

sections.heading: Why it matters at home
sections.body: What you wear at home influences your mood and energy. Staying in pyjamas all day can reinforce a sense of sluggishness, especially when working from home or on days you don't go out. Getting dressed — even very comfortably — marks a mental transition into day mode and often lifts motivation. It's not about forcing yourself, it's about treating yourself with a little care.

sections.heading: Comfortable but presentable
sections.body: Dressing at home doesn't mean sacrificing comfort — there are plenty of pieces that are both cosy and presentable. A well-cut sweatpant, a soft jumper, a good-condition tee, a matching loungewear set. The difference from pyjamas lies in the condition and cut of the pieces. You can be perfectly comfortable in something you wouldn't be embarrassed by if someone rang the doorbell.

sections.heading: Keep a simple routine
sections.body: The ideal is a small, effortless routine: at wake-up, change into comfortable but decent home clothes rather than staying in your nightwear. Keep a few loungewear sets you actually like, clean and in good shape, dedicated to this. No need to overthink it — the goal is simply to feel a little more energised and presentable, for yourself above all, with zero external pressure.

steps: Change out of nightwear at the start of the day
Choose home pieces that are soft but still decent
Keep a few loungewear sets you actually like, clean and ready
Aim for comfortable without full-on slovenly
Do it for your energy, not out of obligation

faq.q: Is it a big deal to stay in pyjamas at home?
faq.a: Not dramatic, but getting dressed — even very comfortably — often helps mood and motivation. One full-rest pyjama day is absolutely fine.

faq.q: Does dressing at home actually change anything?
faq.a: Yes. Swapping nightwear for comfortable but decent clothes marks a mental transition that often supports focus and energy.

title: Dressing to Meet Your Partner's Family
intro: A first meeting with your partner's family deserves a polished, natural outfit. The goal: make a good impression while staying yourself.

sections.heading: Polished and respectful
sections.body: Meeting your partner's family is a moment that counts: a thoughtful outfit shows you're taking it seriously and respect the people involved. Aim for something clean, a notch above your everyday — without going stiff and formal, which creates distance. The idea is to come across as presentable and considerate, not to play a character. A clean, well-put-together outfit already sends a positive signal.

sections.heading: Adapt to the context of the meeting
sections.body: The right formality level depends entirely on the setting: a casual lunch at someone's house calls for something different from a more ceremonial dinner or an outing. Ask your partner for context about the family's vibe, the venue, and the general tone — they're the best source. That information prevents you from being over or underdressed and puts you at ease going in.

sections.heading: Stay yourself
sections.body: The trap would be dressing up as someone else to please: the family is meeting the real person in their son's or daughter's life. Stay true to your style — just in a polished version. Keep a touch that feels like you. Comfort matters too: you'll come across as more natural and relaxed in an outfit you feel good in, and that ease is what makes the best impression.

steps: Aim for polished — one notch above everyday
Ask your partner about the tone and context of the meeting
Match your formality level to the setting
Stay true to your style — don't dress up as someone else
Choose something comfortable so you feel at ease

faq.q: Do you need to dress very formally to meet the in-laws?
faq.a: Not unless it's a ceremonious setting. Aim for natural and polished, adapted to the context. The most important thing is being considerate while staying yourself.

faq.q: How do you know the tone of the meeting?
faq.a: Ask your partner — they know their family, the venue, and the vibe, and will give you the best advice.

title: Choosing Colours Based on Season and Light
intro: Colours don't have the same effect in every season and light. Adapting your palette makes outfits feel more in tune with their surroundings.

sections.heading: Light changes everything
sections.body: The same colour doesn't read the same under bright summer light as under a grey winter sky. Light, bright colours catch summer light well, while deep tones stand out better in the softer light of winter. This isn't a hard rule, but adapting your palette to the season helps outfits feel coherent with their environment.

sections.heading: Seasonal palettes
sections.body: Spring and summer suit light, bright, fresh colours: pastels, whites, luminous tones that respond to strong light and seasonal energy. Autumn and winter call for deeper, warmer colours: bordeaux, bottle green, camel, brown, midnight blue — shades that work in softer, dimmer light. Neutrals, as always, cross every season without issue.

sections.heading: Adapt without depriving yourself
sections.body: These seasonal cues are suggestions, not prohibitions.
